hypothesis: For datasets with heavy-tailed graph size distributions (such as AFLOW), dynamically adjusting the padding budget during training based on the running statistics of recently encountered graph sizes—rather than estimating it once from a fixed random subset at initialization—will reduce the frequency of budget violations and decrease mean combined training time per step by at least 20% compared to the static budget dynamic algorithm, while simultaneously reducing the variance in effective batch sizes and thereby improving final model test RMSE on long-tailed datasets like AFLOW.
